narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical student's 3.21—and your LSAT score of 35, close to the average of 36.70. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 53.1%, tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. This outweighed the positive push from your undergraduate GPA, which strongly suggested you'd pass, as well as supportive influences from your LSAT score, family income, and full-time status. Your Year 1 decile of 3, slightly below the average of 5.71, added a smaller nudge toward the failure prediction, but it wasn't as dominant. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your profile is competitive in GPA and LSAT but stands out negatively in those school decile rankings, which the model weighed heavily for Black women like you navigating similar paths. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model's data-driven assessment based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT prep or academic advising tailored to bar success stories from women of color. Many have turned similar profiles around by focusing on these areas, and reapplying with strengthened deciles could shift the odds in your favor. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
